Facts.text: Supernova observed by Chinese and Egyptians in constellation Lupus
Bishop Bernold flees St Pieterskerk for Utrecht, Netherlands
Albert I, King of Germany, is assassinated by his nephew John of Swabia [the Parricide], for cutting John out of his right to inherit
Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton: The Kingdom of England recognizes the Kingdom of Scotland as an independent state, ending the Wars of Scottish Independence
Ekiho exorcises the Zen temple and its surroundings of an old badger
Danish prince and later KingChristian IIIarrives in Veere, Zeeland
Jacob van Neck's merchant fleet departs for Java, modern day Indonesia
Spanish Queirós Expedition are the first Europeans to discover Espíritu Santo, Vanuatu - although they think they have discovered Terra Australis (southern continent)
Portuguese and Spanish expedition recaptures Salvador (Bahia) from the forces of the Dutch West India Company
Louis XIV and his court inaugurate the Paris Observatory
Boston News-Letter publishes first newspaper advertisement
Acts of Union come into force, uniting England and Scotland to form the Kingdom of Great Britain
Basilica of Our Lady of Guadalupe first opens its doors in Mexico City on the site of two apparitions of the Virgin - now a basilica and the holiest church in Mexico
Brandenburg-Prussia joins the Russian-led anti Swedish alliance by declaring war on Sweden during the Great Northern War
Spanish Catholic missionaries establish Mission San Antonio de Valero (now known as Alamo), the fist of several settlements in what is now San Antonio, Texas
Spain and Austria sign trade treaty
France and Austria sign First Treaty of Versailles alliance - guaranteeing Austrian neutrality in war between Britain and France
Austria and France divide Prussia
British fleet occupies Guadeloupe, West Indies, capturing it from France
German philosopher Adam Weishaupt founds the secret society of the Illuminati
Battle of Crooked Billet: British forces surprise and inflict a heavy defeat on the Pennsylvania militia led by John Lacey in Hatboro, Pennsylvania, during the American Revolution
John Phillips is elected the first mayor of Boston
Belgian parliament accepts railway laws
Austrian  explorer Ida Laura Pfeiffer (48) begins her first trip around world, leaving by boat from Hamburg bound for Rio de Janeiro
The Fraternity of Phi Gamma Delta is founded at Jefferson College in Canonsburg, Pennsylvania
John Geary becomes 1st mayor of San Francisco
First public flushing toilets the 'Monkey Closets' unveiled by George Jennings as part of The Great Exhibition at Hyde Park, London, costing one penny
Great Exhibition opens in the Crystal Palace, at Hyde Park, London
The Philippine peso is introduced into circulation
Amsterdam begins transferring drinking water out of the dunes, a major urban infrastructure development
Argentina adopts its constitution
William Walker, conqueror of Nicaragua, surrenders to the US Navy in Rivas
Battle of Chancellorsville, Virginia, 29,000 injured or died
Battle of Port Gibson: Union victory, Confederates outnumbered about 8,000 to 23,000 (Mississippi)
Confederate congress passes resolution to kill black soldiers
Hospital for the Ruptured and Crippled first opens its doors in New York City, oldest orthopaedic hospital in the United States
The "Confederate National Flag" replaces the "Stars and Bars"
Atlanta campaign in Georgia begins during US Civil War
Union forces clash with Confederates at the Battle of Alexandria, Louisiana (Red River Campaign)
Emperor Franz Joseph opens 5th World's Exposition in Vienna
US issues the world's first government postal card
238 members of the "Whiskey Ring" are accused of anti-US activities, one of the largest corruption scandals in US history
Alexandra Palace in London reopens after burning down in 1873
Bob Rogers is acknowledged as the first American pro sports trainer when he is hired by the NY Athletic Club
First National League baseball game in Philadelphia since 1876, Providence Greys beat Philadelphia Quakers, 4-3
The International Colonial and Export Trade Exposition is held from May 1 to October 1 in Amsterdam
Catcher Moses Walker is acknowledged as the first African-American to play major league baseball joining the Toledo Blue Stockings
Construction begins on Chicago's first skyscraper (10 stories)
Maria "Goeie Mie" Swanenburg sentence to life for killing 27 in Netherlands
Nationwide demonstrations and strikes demanding an 8-hour workday begin in the US
2nd International Congress calls for 1st International Workers Day 1st May 1890 to mark protests in Chicago in 1886
US Quarantine Station opens on Angel Island, San Francisco Bay
World's Columbian Exposition showcasing technological innovations opens in Chicago (original Ferris wheel, first moving walkway)
Scofield Mine disaster: a premature blast collapses a mine tunnel, killing at least 200 in Scofield, Utah
Chicago White Sox outfielder Herm McFarland hits first grand slam in American League history in 19-9 win at home against Detroit; Tigers commit 12 errors
Pan-American Exposition opens in Buffalo, New York (runs till 2 November)
Philadelphia Phillies Johnny Lush (20) pitches no-hitter in 6-0 win over Brooklyn Superbas in Washington Park in Brooklyn, New York; the team's last no-hitter until 1964
Belgium government of De Trooz forms
World's most intense rainfall (2.47 inches in 3 minutes) occurs in Portobelo, Panama
The Beverly Hills Hotel opens on Sunset Boulevard in Beverly Hills, California
Longacre Theatre opens at 220 W 48th Street in New York City
German submarine torpedoes US tanker Gulflight
Belgium-Luxembourg toll tunnel opens
Longest MLB game by innings - Brooklyn Robins tie the Boston Braves, 1-1, in 26 innings
Roermond Football Club (1936 KNVB Cup) is established in Netherlands
Charlie Robertson of the Chicago White Sox pitches a perfect game against the Detroit Tigers
Admiral Paul Koundouriotis becomes President of Greece
British coal miners go on strike
Imperial Airways is the first British airliner to serve cooked meals
Pitcairn Airlines, later Eastern Air Lines, begins service
6 children die and 10 injured by hailstones in Klausenburg, Romania
Fascist Erich Wichman attacks socialist VARA-radio transmitter while drunk
Lei Day begins (a Hawaiian celebration)
Rotterdam soccer club Black White '28 is established (2000 Women's KNVB Cup); declared bankrupt 2004
Farm workers strike begins in East-Groningen, Netherlands
Police kill 19 and injure at least 250 May Day demonstrators in Berlin
The planet Pluto is officially named by 11-year-old English schoolgirl Venetia Burney
Empire State Building opens in New York City as the world's tallest building until the World Trade Center surpasses it in 1970
Norway claims uninhabited Peter I Island in the Bellingshausen Sea near Antarctica
Singer Kate Smith begins her long-running association with CBS radio, various programs continue until 1945
First Suriname union congress held in Paramaribo
Tydings-McDuffie Act: Philippine Legislature accepts the US proposal for independence (realized in 1946)
Canada's 1st silver dollar enters circulation, featuring King George V
Pulitzer Prize awarded to Marjorie Kinnan Rawlings  for "The Yearling"
Summer Olympics (scheduled for Helsinki) are cancelled by the Finnish OC because of WWII
General Mills introduces CheeriOats, an oat-based, ready-to-eat cold cereal; renamed Cherrios in 1945
US savings bonds go on sale to help finance the war effort
Radio Orange (Oranje) urges listeners to defy the Nazi-imposed order requiring Jewish people to wear the Star of David
Food rationing begins in the United States during World War II
German plane sinks the British ship SS Erinpura in the Mediterranean with the loss of 799 lives
SS General Hanns Albin Rauter announces that all Jews will be 'removed' from the occupied Netherlands
Pulitzer prize awarded to American Martin Flavin for his novel "Journey in the Dark"
Surprise attack on Weteringschans, Amsterdam, fails
About 1,000 citizens of Demmin in Germany, commit suicide provoked by occupation by Soviet Red Army
Australian and Dutch troops land on Tarakan, off the coast of Borneo
Radio Budapest, Hungary re-enters shortwave broadcasting after WW II
Seys-Inquart flees to Flensburg
Soviet army reaches Rostock
Mrs Emma Clarissa Clement named "American Mother of Year" by the American Mothers Committee of the Golden Rule Foundation in New York
Start of the three-year Pilbara strike by indigenous Aboriginals in Western Australia for better wages and conditions
The Paris Peace Conference concludes that the islands of the Dodecanese should be returned to Greece by Italy
Cleveland Indians abandon League Park (venue for weekday games) to play all MLB games at Municipal Stadium
General Hoyt Vandenberg, ends his term as the second head of the CIA
Radar for commercial and private planes is first demonstrated
Rear Admiral Roscoe H. Hillenkoetter, becomes the third Director of the CIA
Glenn Taylor, Idaho Senator, arrested in Birmingham, Alabama, for trying to enter a meeting through a door marked "for Negroes"
North Korea proclaims itself Democratic People's Republic of Korea
Athletics' Elmer Valo is the first AL baseball player to hit 2 bases-loaded triples in a game
Gerard Kuiper discovers Nereid, (2nd satellite of Neptune)
Gwendolyn Brooks is the first African American awarded the Pulitzer Prize for poetry for "Annie Allen"
Mayor of Brussels reluctantly bans May Day parade
New marriage laws enforced in People's Republic of China
WJIM (now WLNS) TV channel 6 in Lansing, MI (CBS) begins broadcasting
600,000 march for peace & freedom in Germany
Dutch Reformed Church introduces new church choir
Mr Potato Head first introduced by toy distributor Hasbro
TWA introduces tourist class
US Marines take part in an atomic explosion training in Nevada
Bishops publish Mandement (member socialist org forbidden)
HSA-UWC Forms (Unification Church) (Moonies)
The Hudson Motor Car Company merges with the Nash-Kelvinator Corporation to form the American Motors Corporation
WAPA TV channel 4 in San Juan, PR (NBC/SFN) begins broadcasting
A doctor in Japan reports an "epidemic of an unknown disease of the central nervous system", marking the official discovery of Minamata disease
Flevo Boys Football Club is established in Emmeloord, the Netherlands
US gives Poland credit of $95 million
Vanguard TV-1 booster test reaches 195 km
Ambonese rebellion bombs Ambon, conquers Morotai
Arturo Frondizi sworn in as President of Argentina
West Germany introduces a 5-day work week
India's Bombay state split into Gujarat & Maharashtra states
1st US airplane hijacked to Cuba
Tanganyika granted full internal self-government by Britain
1st French underground nuclear test in the Sahara at Ecker, Algeria
Indonesia takes control of Irian Jaya (west New Guinea) from the Netherlands
Jim Whittaker becomes the first American to summit Mount Everest
First BASIC program runs on a computer at Dartmouth College
Battle of Dong-Yin, a naval conflict between ROC and PRC, takes place.
Stanley Cup, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec: Dick Duff has a goal and 2 assists as Montreal Canadiens beat Chicago Blackhawks, 4-0 in Game 7 to take title 4-3
USSR launches Luna 5; later impacts on Moon
Last British concert by the Beatles at Empire Pool in Wembley, London
Radio RSA, South Africa begins shortwave transmitting
US troops shooting targets in Cambodia
Jelle Zijlstra becomes president of Netherlands Bank
Pulitzer Prize awarded to Bernard Malamud (Fixer)
43 Unification church couples wed in NYC
Houston Astros hurler Don Wilson blanks Reds, 4-0 for second career no-hitter, at Crosley Field in Cincinnati
Pirate Radio Station 259 (England/France) begins transmitting
Trucking magnate Leonard Tose buys NFL Philadelphia Eagles for a professional sports record $16.15m
Amtrak Railroad begins operation
Rolling Stones release single  "Brown Sugar"
Diocletianand Maximian retire from the office of Roman Emperor
Eastern Roman Emperor Arcadius dies, leaving his sonTheodosius IIaged seven as emperor
Christopher Columbusproposes his plan to search for a western route to India in an audience with Spanish monarch,Isabella I. Full support is granted 3 years later, in 1489
Council of Trentresumes, overseen by PopeJulius III
Habsburg EmperorCharles VIand Hungarian nobles including  Sándor Károlyi sign Peace of Szatmar ending rebellion led by Francis II Rákóczi
Publication of "Species Plantarum" byCarl Linnaeus- the formal start date of plant taxonomy adopted by the International Code of Botanical Nomenclature

Publication ofJames Cook's first significant mapping effort, "A New Chart of the River St Laurence, from the Island of Anticosti To the Falls of Richelieu." Later critical to British defeat of the French at Quebec City.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart's opera "The Marriage of Figaro" premieres in Vienna withMozarthimself conducting
Kamehameha I, King of Hawaiʻi defeats Kalanikupule and conquers island of Oʻahu at Battle of Nuʻuanu [approx. date]
Whig Party nominatesHenry Clayas presidential candidate at the Whig Convention in Baltimore
Jacob andWilhelm Grimmpublish the first part of their German Dictionary "Deutsches Wörterbuch" (fully completed 1961)
General Leeorders Confederate troops under T. J. Jackson to Harper's Ferry
Major GeneralBenjamin Butler's Union forces complete the occupation of New Orleans, after facing no resistance
Legendary pitcherCy Youngwins first game played at Cleveland's League Park; Cleveland Spiders 12, Cincinnati Redlegs 3
Seven days after parliament was dissolved,Charles Tupperis sworn in as the 6th Prime Minister of Canada
US AdmiralGeorge Deweycommands, "You may fire when you are ready, Gridley" as the US routs the Spanish fleet at Manila Bay
KingEdward VIIof Great Britain visits Paris, where he is feted in a first step toward improving Anglo-French relations, culminating in the signing of the Entente Cordiale on 8 April, 1904

Yuan Shikai's cabinet replaces China's provisional constitution with a constitutional compact granting the President dictatorial powers over China's military, finances, foreign policy, and the rights of Chinese citizens
British liner Lusitania leaves NY for Liverpool on its last fateful journey
"L'Ordine Nuovo" Italian socialist weekly newspaper established in Turin byAntonio Gramsci, Angelo Tasca andPalmiro Togliatti
British naval officerDavid Beattyis promoted to Admiral of the Fleet
Legendary sluggerBabe Ruthrecords his first HR for the New York Yankees in 6-0 win over his former club, the Boston Red Sox
Adolf Hitlerand Ernst Rohm attempt to break up socialist May Day demonstrates, inviting Nazis from as far away as Nuremberg to take part in the violence
German automobile manufacturers Daimler-Motoren-Gesellschaft and Benz & Cie begin their first joint venture, later merging into Mercedes-Benz
Sun Yat Sen establishes the Republic of China Military Academy on Changzhou Island in Guangdong, first classes held on June 16
Future Baseball Hall of Fame catcherJimmie Foxxmakes his MLB debut at 17 for Philadelphia A's; pinch-hits a single in 9-4 loss v Washington
Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherSatchel Paige(19) debuts in the Negro Southern League for Chattanooga
Ethiopian EmperorHaile Selassieleaves Ethiopia as Italy invades
FBI'sJ. Edgar Hooverarrests gangster Alvin "Creepy" Karpis
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltsigns the Neutrality Act, banning travel on belligerent ships and imposes an arms embargo on warring nations
"Citizen Kane," directed byOrson Wellesand starring himself, Joseph Cotten, and Dorothy Comingore, premieres at the Palace Theater in New York City
Field MarshalBernard Montgomeryappointed British supreme commander
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Auspicia quaedam
Pulitzer Prize for Drama is awarded toRichard RodgersandOscar Hammerstein IIfor their musical "South Pacific"
Future Baseball Hall of Fame sluggerMickey Mantlehits first career home run in 8-3 win v White Sox in Chicago; Minnie Miñoso homers in Sox debut
Future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherBob Fellertosses his record 12th 1-hitter in Indians' 2-0 win v Boston Red Sox at Cleveland Stadium
Larry King's 1st radio broadcast, on station WAHR (1490-AM) in Miami Beach, Florida
Floyd Pattersonscores 11th round KO of Englishman Brian London in Indianapolis; his 4th World Heavyweight Boxing title defence
Russia shoots downFrancis Gary Powers' U-2 spy plane over Sverdlovsk
Fidel Castroannounces there will be no more elections in Cuba
Pulitzer prize awarded toHarper Leefor her novel "To Kill a Mockingbird"
JFKauthorizes Area Redevelopment Act (ARA)
Gloria Steinem's exposé, "A Bunny's Tale", on the working conditions for waitresses (bunnies) at New York City’s famedPlayboy Club, published in "Show" magazine
Anastasio Somoza Debaylebecomes President of Nicaragua
Children's educational television hostFred Rogerstestifies before US Senate Subcommittee on Communications to secure funding for creation of PBS
James Chichester-Clarkis elected as leader of the Unionist party, succeededingTerence O'Neillas the Northern Ireland Prime Minister
Reprise Records releases "Clouds", Canadian singer-songwriterJoni Mitchell's 2nd studio album
"Penny Black," the world's first adhesive postage stamp is issued by the United Kingdom
Batman first appears in Detective Comics #27
Showgirl Linda Lawson is crowned "Miss-Cue" in the Atomic Pageant, after the Operation Cue test is repeatedly delayed by high winds
John I, Count of Hainaut (1246-57), born in Houffalize, Luxembourg (d. 1257)
Rudolf I, King of Germany, Emperor of the Holy Roman Empire (1273-91), born in Limburgh Castle, Sasbach am Kaiserstuhl, Germany (d. 1291)
Magnus VI Lagabuter, King of Norway (1263-80), born in Tønsberg, Norway (d. 1280)
Edmund FitzAlan, 9th Earl of Arundel, English politician, born in Marlborough Castle, Wiltshire, England (d. 1326)
Franciscus Junius, French-Dutch calvinist theologist and vicar, born in Bourges, France (d. 1602)
Michiel Janszoon van Mierevelt, Dutch royal painter, born in Delft, Netherlands (d. 1641)
François de Kinschot, Dutch jurist and chancellor of Brabant, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1654)
Marco da Gagliano, Italian opera composer, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1643)
Johann Adam Schall von Bell, German missionary in China and astronomer, born in Cologne, Germany (d. 1666)
John Haynes, 5th Governor of the Massachusetts Bay Colony, born in Essex, England (d. 1653)
John Woodward, English geologist known for his collection of fossils and physician, born in Derbyshire (d. 1728)
James Stirling, Scottish mathematician (Formula of Stirling), born in this month in Stirlingshire, Scotland (d. 1770)
Jan Hendrik van Kinsbergen, Dutch naval officer and founder of Dutch Marines Corp, born in Doesburg, Netherlands (d. 1819)
Jacob Albright, German-American Christian leader, founder of Albright's People (Evangelical Association), born in Fox Mountain, Pennsylvania (d. 1808)
Benjamin Henry Latrobe, American architect (completed U.S. Capitol), born in Leeds, Yorkshire, England (d. 1820)
Gottfried Rieger, Czech composer, born in Opavice, Silesia (d. 1855)
George Guest, English organist and composer, born in Broseley, England (d. 1831)
Jacob-Joseph-Balthasar Martinn, Flemish composer, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1836)
Thomas Aloysius Dornin, American naval officer, born in Ireland (d. 1874)
Aleksey Stepanovich Khomyakov, Russian poet, born in Moscow (d. 1860) [NS 13/05]
John B. Magruder, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Port Royal, Virginia (d. 1871)
José Amador de los Ríos, Spanish historian and poet, born in Baena, Spain (d. 1878)
William Steele, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Albany, New York (d. 1885)
Charles Samvei Bovy-Lysberg, Swiss pianist and composer, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1873)
George Inness, American landscape painter (Delaware Water Gap), born in Newburgh, New York (d. 1894)
José de Alencar, Brazilian writer (O Guarani), born in Fortaleza, Ceará, Empire of Brazil (d. 1877)
Guido Gezelle, Flemish priest and poet, born in Brugge, Belgium (d. 1899)
Mother [Mary Harris] Jones, American labor organizer (cofounded Industrial Workers of the World), born in Cork City, Ireland (d. 1930)
Emily Stowe, Canadian suffragist and first woman licensed to practise medicine in Canada, born in Norwich Township, Oxford County, Ontario (d. 1903)
Alfred Napoleon Alexander "Natti" Duffié, French-American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Paris, France (d. 1880)
Hilaire de Chardonnet, French industrialist and inventor (rayon), born in Besançon, France (d. 1924)
[Robert] Lawson Tait, Scottish-born pioneering surgeon performed first salpingectomy for ectopic pregnancy, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1899)
Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn, Governor General of Canada (1911-16), born in Buckingham Palace, London (d. 1942)
Laza Lazarević, Serbian writer (Prvi put s ocem na jutrenje) and psychiatrist, born in Šabac, Serbia (d. 1891)
Santiago Ramón y Cajal, Spanish histologist (central nervous system) and neuroscientist (Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1906), born in Petilla de Aragón, Navarre, Spain (d. 1934)
Charles Lee Williams, English composer and organist, born in Winchester (d. 1935)
Cecilia Beaux, American portrait painter, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1942)
Theo van Gogh, Dutch art dealer and brother of Vincent van Gogh, born in Groot-Zundert, Netherlands (d. 1891)
Anthony Johnson Showalter, American composer, born in Cherry Grove, Virginia (d. 1924)
Bohuslav Jeremiáš, Czech composer, born in Řestoky, Bohemia, Austrian Empire (d. 1918)
Willem Johannes Leyds, Dutch-South Africa lawyer and diplomat (State Secretary of the South African Republic), born in Magelang, Dutch East Indies (d. 1940)
Hugo Alfvén, Swedish violinist, conductor, and composer (Midsommarvaka - Midsummer Vigil), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1960)
Sidónio Pais, Portuguese politician (4th President of Portugal, 66th Prime Minister of Portugal), born in Caminha, Portugal (d. 1918)
Romaine Brooks [Beatrice Romaine Goddard], American painter, born in Rome, Italy (d. 1970)
Konrad Weiss, German writer and poet (Tantum dic verbo - Heart of Words), born in Gaildorf, Württemberg, German Empire (d. 1940)
Pierre Teilhard de Chardin, French philosopher and paleontologist (The Phenomenon of Man), born in Orcines, Auvergne, France (d. 1955)
Felipe Boero, Argentine composer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1958)
Francis Curzon, 5th Earl Howe, British politician, naval officer and auto racer (1931 Le Mans), born in Mayfair, London (d. 1964)
Alan Cunningham, British general and last High Commissioner of Palestine (1945-48), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1983)
John Evans, English cricket batsman (1 Test) and author ('he Escaping Club), born in Newtown, Hampshire (d. 1960)
Howard Barlow, American conductor (Voice of Firestone), born in Plain City, Ohio (d. 1972)
Florimond Cornellie, Belgian sailor (Olympic gold 6-metre class 1920), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1978)
Leo Sowerby, American organist and composer (Pulitzer Prize for Music, 1946), born in Grand Rapids, Michigan (d. 1968)
Herbert Backe, German Nazi politician (Reich Minister for Food and Agriculture), born in Batumi, Kutais Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1947)
J. Lawton Collins, American general, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1987)
Mark W. Clark, American general, born in Madison Barracks, Sackets Harbor, New York (d. 1984)
David Tobey, American Basketball Hall of Fame referee (1918-45; first game with a 3-man officiating crew), born in New York, New York (d. 1988)
Eugene R. Black, American President of World Bank (1953-62), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1992)
Ignazio Silone, Italian novelist (Fontamara; Pane e vino), anti-fascist activist, and politician, born in Pescina, Italy (d. 1978)
Antal Szerb, Hungarian author (The Pendragon Legend; Journey by Moonlight), and literary scholar (History of World Literature), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1945)[1]
Heinz Roemheld, American film composer (Yankee Doodle Dandy; Ruby Gentry), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1985)
Slats Gill, Basketball Hall of Fame coach (36 seasons Oregon State 1928-64), born in Salem, Oregon (d. 1966)
Chris 'Red' Cagle, American College Football Hall of Fame halfback (All American 1927, 28, 29; Army; NFL: NY Giants, Brooklyn Dodgers), born in DeRidder, Louisiana (d. 1942)
Leila Hyams, American actress (Big House, Ruggles of Red Gap), born in New York City (d. 1977)
Nikolai Tikhonov, Soviet politician (Chairman of the Council of Ministers of the Soviet Union 1980-85), born in Kharkiv, Kharkov Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1997)
Paul Desruisseaux, Canadian lawyer and politician (Senator for Wellington, Quebec), born in Sherbrooke, Quebec (d. 1982)

Horst Schumann, German Nazi physician who oversaw concentration camp x-ray sterilization and gas chamber murder of Jews, as well as euthanasia centers to kill feeble and ill people, born in Halle, Province of Saxony, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1983)
Hayes Alvis, American jazz bassist and tuba player (Jelly Roll Morton; Duke Ellington; Louis Armstrong), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1972)
Kate Smith, American singer ("God Bless America"; "When The Moon Comes Over The Mountain"), radio star, and NHL Philadelphia Flyers good luck charm, born in Greenville, Virginia (d. 1986)
Oliver Hill, American civil rights attorney (Presidential Medal of Freedom 1999), born in Richmond, Virgnia (d. 2007)
Giovannino Guareschi, Italian writer (Don Camillo, Peppone), born in Roccabianca, Emilia, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1968)
Ethel Jane Cain, British telephonist and original UK Speaking Clock voice, born in England (d. 1996)
George Melachrino, English composer and easy listeining orchestra leader ("Autumn Concerto", "Starlight Roof Waltz"), born in London (d. 1965)
Yiannis Ritsos, Greek poet and political activist, born in Monemvasia, Laconia, Greece (d. 1990)[1]

Cliff Battles, American College/Pro Football HOF halfback (West Virginia Wesleyan College; 3 x First-Team All-Pro; NFL rushing yards leader 1932, 37; Boston Braves/Redskins, Washington Redskins), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 1981)
Anna Pollak, English mezzo-soprano, born in Manchester (d. 1996)
Felipe Padilla de León, Filipino composer, born in General Tinio, Nueva Ecija, Philippine Islands (d. 1992)
Otto Kretschmer, German U-Boat commander, born in Heidau, Neisse, Province of Silesia, Prussia, German Empire (d. 1998)
Winthrop Rockefeller, American entrepreneur, politician (Governor of Arkansas, 1967-71), and philanthropist, born in New York City (d. 1973
Balraj Sahni [Yudhishthir], Indian Bollywood actor (Dharti Ke Lal), born in Rawalpindi, Punjab (d. 1973)
Louis Nye, American comedian and actor (Curb Your Enthusiasm; The Steve Allen Plymouth Show; The Beverly Hillbillies - "Sonny Drysdale"), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2005)
Marvin 'Bud' Ward, American golfer (1939, 1941 US Amateur), born in Elma, Washington (d. 1968)
Walter Susskind, Czech conductor and pianist, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1980)
John Henry Lewis, American boxer (World Light-Heavyweight champion 1935-38), born in Phoenix, Arizona (d. 1974)
Archie Williams, American athlete (Olympic gold 400m 1936), born in Oakland, California (d. 1993)
Hanns Martin Schleyer, German industrialist, born in Offenburg, Grand Duchy of Baden, German Empire (d. 1977)
Glenn Ford, Canadian-American actor (Cade's County, Big Heat, Midway), born in Sainte-Christine-d'Auvergne, Quebec, Canada (d. 2006)
Ahron Soloveichik, Orthodox Jewish rabbi, born in Khislavichi, Russia (d. 2001)
Danielle Darrieux, French stage and screen actress (Alexander the Great; Mayerling; 8 Women), born in Bordeaux, France (d. 2017)
Giovanni "John" Beradino, American MLB infielder (St. Louis Browns, Cleveland Indians) and actor (General Hospital), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1996)
Gersh Budker, Russian physicist who invented electron cooling, born in Murafa, Ukraine (d. 1977)
Al Zarilla, American baseball outfielder (MLB All Star 1948; St. Louis Browns), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1996)
Daniel "Dan" O'Herlihy, Irish actor (Twin Peals, Fail Safe, Last Starfighter, Robocop), born in County Wexford, Ireland (d. 2005)
[Prabodh] "Manna" Dey, Indian playback singer, music director, and musician, born in Calcutta, Bengal Presidency, British India (d. 2013)
Floyd "Candy" Johnson, American jazz saxophonist (Andy Kirk; Count Basie; Bill Doggett), and educator, born in Madison, Illinois (d. 1981)
Frank Brian, American basketball guard (NBA All-Star 1951, 52; Fort Wayne Pistons), born in Zachary, Louisiana (d. 2017)
Joseph Heller, American novelist (Catch-22, 1963 Arts & Letters Award), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1999)
Art Fleming [Fazzin], American actor, and TV game show host (Jeopardy, 1964-75, 1978-79), born in New York City (d. 1995)
Big Maybelle [Mabel Smith], American R&B singer ("A Whole Lotta Shakin' Goin' On", "Candy"), born in Jackson, Mississippi (d. 1972)
Dennis Main-Wilson, British TV and radio producer (Marty, Till Death Us Do Part), born in Dulwich, London (d. 1997)
Earl George, American composer and pedagogue, born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1994)
Enriko Josif, Serbian composer and pedagogue (Lyric Symphony), born in Belgrade, Yugoslavia (d. 2003)
Terry Southern, American writer and screenwriter (Dr. Strangelove, Easy Rider), born in Alvarado, Texas (d. 1995)

Chuck Bednarik, Pro/College Football HOF center/linebacker (Uni of Pennsylvania; NFL C'ship 1949, 60; 10 × First-team All-Pro; 8 × Pro Bowl; Philadelphia Eagles), born in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania (d. 2015)
Peter Lax, Hungarian-American mathematician and the first applied mathematician to win the Abel Prize (2005), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2025)[1]
Bernard Vukas, Croatian soccer forward (59 caps, Yugoslavia; Hajduk Split; Olympic silver 1948, 52), born in Zagreb, Croatia (d. 1983)
Billy Byers, American jazz trombonist and arranger, born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1996)

Gary Bertini [Shloyme Golergant], Romanian-Israeli composer, and conductor (Israel Chamber Orchestra, 1965-75; Jerusalem Symphony, 1978-86; Israeli Opera, 1988-97; Tokyo Metropolitan Symphony, 1998- 2005), born in Bricheva, Romania (d. 2005)
Greta Andersen, Danish swimmer (Olympic gold 100m freestyle, silver 4×100m freestyle 1948), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 2023)
Israr Ali, Pakistan cricket all-rounder (4 Tests; member of Pakistan's first Test team that played against India 1952–53), born in Jalandhar, Punjab, India (d. 2016)
